reasoning: The injustice is obvious because the enforcement is explicitly selective and hypocritical. While the rule itself is standard, enforcing it against one employee while the manager and peers openly violate it for personal gain constitutes clear unfairness that a reasonable person would immediately recognize.

The injustice is obvious because the user faces a bureaucratic double bind: the authority mandates software that is technically incompatible with the hardware the same authority provided, while refusing to upgrade the hardware. No reasonable person would defend penalizing an employee for failing to perform a technically impossible task caused by the employer's own resource allocation.

Retroactively reducing commission rates for work that was already completed and closed under a prior agreement is viscerally unfair and effectively constitutes wage theft. No reasonable person would defend a policy that changes the compensation terms for a specific task after the employee has already performed it.

The rigid application of a 'nuclear family only' definition to an employee raised by their aunt, combined with the explicit lack of a human override mechanism, constitutes an obvious bureaucratic injustice. A reasonable person would agree that a system unable to recognize a functional parent-child relationship for bereavement purposes is defectively rigid.

The injustice is obvious because the worker is placed in an impossible double bind: meeting the mandatory quota requires violating the mandatory safety rules. No reasonable person would defend a system that demands performance metrics which are physically impossible to achieve without committing a fireable safety offense.

The injustice is obvious because the user is being held to a different standard than peers with identical roles solely based on their home address, without any business justification. A reasonable person would view forcing one employee to commute to an empty office to take Zoom calls, while exempting others simply because they live in the suburbs, as arbitrary and punitive.

The injustice is obvious because the rule was changed secretly and enforcement is immediate, making compliance impossible for those who relied on the previous policy. No reasonable person defends secret retroactive rule changes, as fair notice is a fundamental requirement of justice.

The enforcement is obviously unfair because the rule has been effectively abandoned (50% violation rate), making sudden, targeted enforcement against one employee arbitrary. A reasonable person would agree that punishing one person to 'make an example' regarding a rule that is openly ignored by half the staff constitutes unjust selective application.

The application of the rule is obviously unfair because compliance is impossible: the user cannot see the limits they are being punished for violating due to a broken link. A reasonable person would agree that enforcing a secret or inaccessible standard while refusing to provide feedback constitutes a clear procedural injustice.

The application of the rule is obviously dysfunctional and unfair. A system where employees cannot warn of project disasters for fear of being disciplined by a 'random' bot that flags trivialities like coffee complaints is a clear failure of reasonable enforcement.
